rationales.stimulation: He treated the "packaging I didn't recognize" as a reason to reject the cheaper option, avoiding the unfamiliar rather than trying it.
rationales.security: He spent "twenty minutes" comparing brands and chose the expensive familiar one, prioritizing predictability over saving money.
"I've spent three years building a buffer" and the thought of the loan hitting his credit report makes his "chest tight," so he protects financial stability first.
rationales.benevolence: Even though "He's family" and "he's trying," he lets a "hard wall" go up instead of taking on risk to help his cousin.

rationales.stimulation: He treats the Jeddah assignment as "gambling what already works," showing reluctance toward a new and uncertain move.
rationales.security: He delays on a promotion track because "staying here" preserves the arrangement that already feels dependable.
"Meal-prepped for the whole week," replaced expired first aid supplies, and checked flashlight batteries so "Tomorrow feels manageable because today I made it so."
He checked the supply order "three times" because the idea of "something going wrong, even something small" feels like standing "on a ledge."
He insists he is not holding on too tight because other people "let go too easily and then act surprised when things fall apart," framing vigilance as protection against loss.

rationales.achievement: He ties his style to performance, saying "things don't fall through" because he checks and follows up, presenting himself as the reason the team runs well.
rationales.security: His defense of being careful centers on preventing failures: "things would fall through constantly" if no one kept checking.
rationales.benevolence: He points out that he helped Faisal cancel his cards and frames his follow-up as taking care of the people around him, not just himself.

rationales.security: He has "six months of expenses saved" yet keeps running worst-case scenarios because he still feels that "the floor could always open."

rationales.self_direction: She celebrates students debating instead of waiting for the "right" answer, encouraging independent thinking rather than rote compliance.
rationales.universalism: The discussion centers on a "coastal development project," and she values that students engage with a broader public issue instead of treating it as someone else's problem.

rationales.self_direction: After being told to "tone down the activism," she just "nodded and said I'd think about it," yielding ground instead of openly defending her approach.
rationales.conformity: She treats the principal's warning as "just politics" and responds with a polite nod, adapting her behavior to institutional expectations.
rationales.universalism: She pushes back on calling ocean pollution "foreign problems" by insisting "the Arabian Sea is not foreign to Kerala," tying the lesson to wider ecological harm.

rationales.self_direction: She decides to "soften my approach" and teach in ways that "don't raise red flags," limiting her own preferred style to fit the institution.
rationales.security: Her strategy is driven partly by staying in place: "if I get pushed out" the students lose even this imperfect version, so preserving her position matters.
rationales.conformity: She plans to be "strategic" and avoid "red flags," showing willingness to work within the school's constraints rather than confront them head-on.
rationales.universalism: She keeps prioritizing students asking questions about "their own coastline," holding onto the public-interest content even while changing tactics.

rationales.tradition: She skipped the Sunday drive they have made "every Sunday since before the kids were born," and describes it as feeling like she "broke something."
rationales.benevolence: She stayed home knowing "Papa sets the table for all of us" and felt sick hearing him "putting the plates away," showing she failed people she usually shows up for.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps insisting "it's just what we do" even while wondering whether it is "just fear of doing something different from what I know," so she resists choosing a different path.
rationales.hedonism: She keeps making the trip "even when it's pouring rain or one of them is sick" and pulls Hugo from football, sacrificing comfort and ease.
rationales.tradition: She defends the weekly visits as "what we do" and refuses to be "the one who lets that happen" when the family table might shrink.
rationales.benevolence: Her main concern is that "My parents eat alone" if she stops showing up, so she prioritizes keeping close family connected.

rationales.tradition: "Four generations of women and a potato dish" and "The recipe box isn't just recipes" show her treating inherited family practices as something to carry forward.
rationales.benevolence: She brings Lea into the kitchen to "help me stir the bechamel" so the meal becomes an act of caring for and teaching her child.

rationales.security: The weekly rhythm of Sunday lunch and "of course, that's what we do" gives her a stable routine that feels reassuring again.
rationales.tradition: She centers family customs like Papa's cassoulet and the vineyard story, noting these are "details that disappear if nobody passes them on."
rationales.benevolence: She is pleased Hugo wants to return next week and treats gathering the family together as something worth sustaining for the people closest to her.

rationales.self_direction: She wonders whether staying home is "just fear of doing something different from what I know," suggesting she is not choosing freely so much as avoiding departure from the family script.
rationales.stimulation: The possibility of working again is framed as "something different from what I know," and that unfamiliarity itself helps keep her from pursuing it.
rationales.hedonism: She admits she has "started counting the hours until bedtime" yet keeps choosing the more draining path over anything restorative for herself.
rationales.achievement: Even though "I was good at it" and had colleagues, she pushes that professional part of herself aside instead of pursuing it.
rationales.security: Being home when the children return and never having them come home to "an empty house" is presented as the safer, more predictable arrangement.
rationales.conformity: The loudest guide is the inherited voice of "Maman, and Grand-mere before her," and she measures herself against that expected model.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly grounds her choice in how "My mother did all of that" and how the women before her organized family life the same way.
rationales.benevolence: She wants to be "here when they come home," cook for them, know their teachers, and build her days around caring for the children.

rationales.hedonism: She says the extra time will come "From sleep, probably" or from the one evening she usually keeps for herself, giving up rest and leisure.
rationales.tradition: She volunteers because the procession was part of what her parents did as children and fears that without action "it just disappears."
rationales.benevolence: She would rather be tired than have to explain to Hugo why "the lanterns aren't happening anymore," making the effort for family and close community.
rationales.universalism: She signs up to organize a parish event because "someone has to," taking responsibility for preserving a shared community tradition beyond her own household.
